title: Creating Tables (CREATE TABLE)
description: Build the box before you put things in it.

slides.title: The Origin Story 🏗️
slides.content:
# Where do tables come from?

Before you can `SELECT`, `INSERT`, or `UPDATE`... you physically have to build the structural database table itself! 

This is called the **Schema** (the blueprint).

slides.title: The CREATE Command 🔨
slides.content:
## Building the box

We use the **`CREATE TABLE`** command, followed by the name we want to give it.

Then, inside parentheses `()`, we meticulously explicitly define every single column this table will possess, and exactly what *type* of data that specific column requires.

quizData.question: What is essentially being accomplished when you execute `CREATE TABLE`?

options.text: You are filling existing tables with new data
You are literally defining the permanent structural blueprint detailing exactly which columns natively exist to hold future data
You are asking the database for permission to log in

quizData.explanation: You cannot inject records (INSERT) into a column that doesn't explicitly structurally exist yet natively inside the core database layout.

slides.title: The Syntax 📝
slides.content:
## Writing it out

A basic table looks like this:
```sql
CREATE TABLE products (
  id INT,
  name VARCHAR(50),
  price DECIMAL
);
```
Notice the commas! We declare the Column Name, followed immediately by its Data Type.

quizData.question: When defining columns inside `CREATE TABLE ()`, what invariably follows the actual name of the column?

options.text: The strict Data Type the column is legally permitted to hold (like 'INT' for Integer numbers)

quizData.explanation: You must establish the rules natively. 'This column is named age, and it is strictly an Integer.'
